title: Spain's Slower Side
content:
It is a slow February day in Trujillo, Spain. At the center of the town's Plaza Mayor, a handful of visitors gaze up at the statue of Francisco Pizarro. He was born and raised here before violently subduing the Inca empire in the early 16th century. From this little-known corner of western Spain, Pizarro and other conquistadores left for the New World. They eventually returned with gold and silver to erect the stone-built palaces and churches that still stand haughtily around the square.

Just a few years ago, meeting an American or any foreign visitor on the road in Extremadura would have been a rare occurrence. In a region the size of Switzerland, with a population of barely a million, you were less likely to encounter a tour group than a troop of pigs eating acorns. These days, however, the situation is changing. As Spain's tourist industry has grown, savvy visitors have begun trading oversaturated destinations like Barcelona for this type of lesser-known spot. Extremadura, located 180 miles from Madrid, offers the authentic Spanish culture they crave but with much more peace and quiet.

My own experience mirrors this broader trend. Throughout the 1990s, I lived on Ibiza, luxuriating in the pre-internet pleasures of a place that had not yet hit the big time with tourism. When relentless development, environmental depletion, and excessive traffic began to take their toll on the island, I decided to leave. Extremadura was initially just a pit stop on my road trip across the Iberian Peninsula. However, what I found there was simply astounding.

I had never been anywhere in southern Europe where nature was so diverse and plentiful. The extent of the built environment was merely a scattering of towns and villages amid vast tracts of wilderness. Remarkably, the impact of tourism on all of this was still minimal. Its traditional rural lifestyle remained a living, breathing relic of Old Europe. The southern province of Badajoz, with its unfurling carpet of olive groves and vineyards, felt like a crowd-free extension of Andalusia.

My friends initially warned me that Extremadura would be a difficult place to travel in and relocate to, claiming it was dry and desolate. By European standards, the region certainly felt remote and underpopulated, and it still does today. Yet, the intrepid few who do make it here are counting their blessings. They discover a profound connection to the land and a slower, more deliberate pace of life. Ultimately, Extremadura represents one of the last true frontiers of unspoiled European travel.
